author: @gidi1899
text:
I like this channel.
I have a question:
what about the tooth profiles ?!
if you zoom in on the tooth shape, 
it's the tooth - tooth connection that relates to allowed torques, right ?!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's an involute tooth profile of module 2 mate - fairly standard really - though i did move the pitch angle from 20 to 30 normally it ranges between 14.5 and 25 thought 20 is favoured

replies.author: @gidi1899
replies.text:
​@ThinkingandTinkering thanks :)

following your lead,
just saw another video explaining the involute tooth.
I'm concerned with pressure transfer path's weak-points that are solved by shape selection.

and the involute path allows a smooth change in material through the pressure area.
but there is a difference on the pressure path going beween the 3 gears, to compensate for the change in the size of the pressure area in tooth-tooth contact.

I just wonder if we can change the tooth profile of gears with a difference of 1 in tooth count, and maybe use same size gears, can we create an even pressure path?!

author: @mrdewilliams
text: I just visualized an interesting clock, with these gear sets nested 3 deep inside each other (concentric) to drive seconds, minutes, and hours.  I would just need to figure out how to temporarily disengage the hands to set the time.  Is this the direction you are thinking with your clock?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes it is mate exactly - i can see two options on moving the hands - either friction fit or dog clutches

replies.author: @mrdewilliams
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Dog clutches id a novel idea.  I loke that.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @mrdewilliams to be honest mate me too lol - i was thinking of having the selector forks on a single arm so it was a flip switch to disengage the gears

replies.author: @mrdewilliams
@ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @mrdewilliams the next itiration is going to be motor driven mate - a few folks have asked about that so i will use this one to demonstrate motor drives and control then back to weights and springs lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @TroyMackay
text:
We're really wasting a lot of potential gear ratio by not making use of that huge drive gear. I posted a remix of the previous model to demonstrate, will try to give this one a similar treatment, should make an even bigger difference.
It's identical in terms of mechanical simplicity, and not a huge leap mathematically. I know you're aiming for a specific gear ratio, but this also means we don't need such large gears to achieve the same ratio.
Alas, both 59 and 61 are prime, so no good for your clock, but I haven't really explored cycloidal arrangements so might be fun to see how low we can go.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I saw the spur gear differential you did on on printables - very job and I agree with you thinking - but I am not sure which one you mean do you have a link I would love to see it - cheers

replies.author: @TroyMackay
replies.text: Sure, "Yet Another Perpetual Wedge" on Thingiverse thing:6813916 a remix of your "The Perpetual Wedge" (I don't think the first reply went through with the link)

replies.author: @TroyMackay
replies.text: Sorry, I'm not having much luck with the replies. It's on the site beginning with "T" in your description who shall not be named.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i found it! nice job and thanks for the video link - you are right in your comments and i fixed that issue in this version - i had to - it's going to be a clock - anyway the outer gears now rotate concentrically and to get it to work with out jamming I changed the pitch angle on the drive gear from 20 to 30 and remodeled the tooth profile of the smaller ring gear by removing the topmost 1mm from the tooth - that's probably more detail than most folks want - but you know what you are doing lol
